cases.title: Drowning in a Toddler
cases.presentation: A 2-year-old child (12 kg) was found floating face-down in a backyard swimming pool. Estimated submersion time is 3–4 minutes. The child was pulled from the water by a bystander who cleared the airway. The child is unresponsive with agonal respirations and a palpable but very slow pulse. The pool water temperature was 26°C (79°F).
cases.question: What is the MOST appropriate initial resuscitation approach?
options: A: Start chest compressions immediately and apply AED
B: Provide rescue breaths first, then begin CPR with ventilations (A-B-C approach)
C: Withhold resuscitation — the child is hypothermic and likely non-survivable
D: Start an epinephrine drip and transport without CPR
cases.explanation: Drowning is a hypoxic arrest — ventilation is the priority. Rescue breaths should be provided first to oxygenate the brain and heart, then CPR with a compression-to-ventilation ratio that emphasizes breaths (30:2). The child has a pulse but is in severe bradycardia with respiratory failure. Hypothermia from cold water is common and should not deter aggressive resuscitation.
cases.clinicalPearl: In drowning, always provide breaths before compressions — the primary problem is lack of oxygen, not cardiac dysfunction. Cold water drowning may be protective.

cases.title: Anaphylaxis Progressing to Arrest
cases.presentation: A 5-year-old child (18 kg) with a known peanut allergy accidentally ingested peanut butter at a birthday party. Within 10 minutes, the child developed urticaria, facial swelling, wheezing, and then became unresponsive. EMS finds the child apneic with a weak pulse of 50 bpm and a blood pressure of 40/20 mmHg. An epinephrine auto-injector was administered by the family at the scene.
cases.question: The family administered epinephrine auto-injector (0.15 mg IM). What additional IMMEDIATE treatment is needed?
options: A: Diphenhydramine 18 mg IV and observe
B: Begin bag-mask ventilation, prepare for intubation, start IV epinephrine, and give a 20 mL/kg fluid bolus
C: Administer methylprednisolone and wait for auto-injector to work
D: Apply AED and deliver a shock
cases.explanation: This child has anaphylactic shock progressing to cardiac arrest. The auto-injector dose may have been insufficient. Immediate priorities: secure the airway (bag-mask ventilation, prepare for intubation due to airway edema), IV epinephrine for hemodynamic support, and aggressive fluid resuscitation for the massive vasodilation and capillary leak of anaphylaxis.
cases.clinicalPearl: Anaphylaxis causes massive vasodilation and capillary leak — fluid boluses of 20 mL/kg may need to be repeated. The auto-injector provides initial IM epinephrine, but IV epinephrine is needed for cardiac arrest.

cases.title: Trauma Arrest in a Pedestrian
cases.presentation: A 9-year-old child (28 kg) is brought to the trauma bay by EMS after being struck by a car while crossing the street. The child was unresponsive at the scene. EMS started CPR. On arrival: no pulse, no respiratory effort. Bilateral breath sounds are absent. The chest is hyper-resonant to percussion on the right. The team initiates CPR.
cases.question: What is the MOST appropriate immediate intervention alongside CPR?
options: A: Continue CPR and obtain a chest CT scan
B: Perform bilateral needle chest decompression, prepare blood products, and continue CPR
C: Give 3 liters of normal saline rapidly
cases.explanation: Bilateral absent breath sounds with hyper-resonance after blunt trauma indicate bilateral tension pneumothorax — a reversible cause of arrest. Bilateral needle decompression should be performed immediately without waiting for imaging. Blood products (not crystalloid) should be prepared rapidly for suspected hemorrhagic shock. CPR continues throughout.
cases.clinicalPearl: In pediatric trauma arrest, always think 'blood, air, and cut' — hemorrhage (blood products), pneumothorax (decompress), and tamponade (surgical intervention).
